walkthrough.title: Build the Map
walkthrough.text: Let's get Clans of Caledonia set up. First, we need to build the modular map. Grab the four double-sided hex map tiles. Each tile shows a different region of Scotland with forests, mountains, lochs, and rivers. For your first game, use the recommended side for each tile as shown in the rulebook. Arrange them in a two-by-two grid to form the full map.

walkthrough.title: Place the Port Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the port bonus tiles. These small tiles go on the designated port spaces around the edges of the map. Each port bonus gives a reward when you place a unit next to it. Shuffle them face down and place one on each port space, then flip them face up. These vary each game, so take a moment to see what bonuses are available.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Market Board
walkthrough.text: Now place the market board next to the map. This is the heart of the economy. You will see tracks for each trade good: wheat, milk, wool, cheese, bread, and whisky. Place the price markers on their starting positions as shown on the board. Remember, buying goods raises their price and selling lowers it. The market is dynamic and shared by all players.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the nine scoring tiles. Shuffle them face down, then randomly draw five. Place one tile on each of the five round spaces on the scoring track. Return the rest to the box without looking. Each tile shows a different condition that awards bonus victory points at the end of that round. Take a moment to read all five so everyone knows what is coming.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Export Contracts
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the export contract tiles face down. Deal a number of contracts face up based on your player count. For four players, reveal eight contracts. Place them in a row next to the map where everyone can see them. These contracts show what goods you need to deliver and what rewards you get, including victory points and bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cotton and Tobacco
walkthrough.text: Set out the imported goods tokens: cotton and tobacco. These cannot be produced by any clan. You can only get them by fulfilling certain export contracts that grant them as bonuses or by buying them on the market. Place these tokens in a supply near the market board.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Clan
walkthrough.text: Now for the exciting part. Shuffle the nine clan tiles and deal two to each player. Each clan has a unique power that changes how you play. Read both of your options carefully. Some clans give economic bonuses, others help with expansion or production. Pick one clan and return the other to the box. For your first game, Clan MacDonald and Clan Campbell are great beginner-friendly choices.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Player Board
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board. This board holds all your units before you deploy them to the map. You will see spaces for workers, fields, pastures, and processing buildings. Your shipping level track is also here. Every unit on your board has a cost printed beneath it. When you deploy a unit, you pay that cost plus additional distance fees.

walkthrough.title: Populate Your Player Board
walkthrough.text: Place all of your wooden units on the matching spaces of your player board. Woodcutters, miners, and fishermen go on the worker row. Sheep and cattle go on the pasture row. Grain fields go on the field row. Bakeries, dairies, and distilleries go on the processing row. Merchants go on the merchant row. Everything starts on your board and moves to the map when you deploy.

walkthrough.title: Receive Starting Money
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their starting money. The amount depends on your clan. Your clan tile tells you exactly how much you start with. Place your money tokens where you can see them. Money is public information in Clans of Caledonia, so everyone should be able to see how much each player has.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Units
walkthrough.text: In reverse turn order, each player places one starting unit on the map for free. Then in reverse turn order again, each player places a second starting unit. These initial placements do not cost any money, but you must follow normal placement rules. Pick hexes that are close together and near resources you want to produce. Your starting positions shape your entire game.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Turn order in Clans of Caledonia is based on money. The player with the least money goes first. If there is a tie, the player who was earlier in the previous turn order goes first. Set up the turn order track accordingly. This order will update at the start of each new round based on current cash. Having less money means you get first pick of actions.

walkthrough.title: Final Check
walkthrough.text: Let's do a quick final check. Map assembled with port bonuses? Market board with prices at starting positions? Five scoring tiles placed on the round track? Export contracts face up? Every player has a clan, a player board full of units, starting money, and two units on the map? Turn order set? Great, you are ready to play Clans of Caledonia.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure
walkthrough.text: Clans of Caledonia is played over five rounds. Each round has four phases: Preparation, Action, Production, and Scoring. The Action phase is where you spend most of your time, taking turns performing one action at a time until everyone passes. Let's walk through each phase and action in detail.

walkthrough.title: Preparation Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, first update the turn order. The player with the least money goes first. Then refill the export contract display to the correct number. Finally, collect any round-specific bonuses your clan might provide. In round one, you skip this phase since everything is already set up.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: During the Action phase, players take turns in turn order. On your turn, you must perform exactly one action or pass. Once you pass, you are done for the round. You cannot un-pass. The six possible actions are: Trade, Place a Unit, Upgrade Shipping, Fulfill an Export Contract, Take an Export Contract, and Pass. Let's go through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action: Trade
walkthrough.text: The Trade action lets you buy or sell goods on the market. You can make multiple transactions in a single Trade action, buying and selling different goods freely. When you buy a good, pay the current market price and take the good from the supply. Then move that good's price marker up one step. When you sell a good, return it to the supply, collect the current price, then move the marker down one step. Prices fluctuate with every transaction.

walkthrough.title: Action: Place a Unit
walkthrough.text: The Place a Unit action lets you deploy one unit from your player board onto the map. You pay two costs: the base cost printed on your player board beneath the unit, plus a distance cost. Distance is measured from your nearest existing unit on the map. Adjacent hexes cost zero distance. Each hex farther away costs extra money. Rivers and lochs block movement unless you have upgraded your shipping level.

walkthrough.title: Placement Distance and Cost
walkthrough.text: Let's break down distance cost more clearly. If you place a unit on a hex directly adjacent to one of your existing units, the distance cost is zero. For each additional hex of distance, you pay more. The exact cost per step depends on the terrain. Placing adjacent is always cheapest, so expanding outward from your existing network saves you a lot of money. Plan your expansion carefully.

walkthrough.title: Unit Types: Workers
walkthrough.text: There are three types of workers: woodcutters, miners, and fishermen. Woodcutters must be placed on forest hexes and produce wood. Miners must be placed on mountain hexes and produce money during production. Fishermen must be placed on river or loch-adjacent hexes and produce food. Workers are your cheapest units to deploy and help you expand across the map quickly.

walkthrough.title: Unit Types: Fields and Pastures
walkthrough.text: Fields produce grain and must be placed on grassland hexes. Pastures hold either sheep or cattle. Sheep produce wool and cattle produce milk. These raw goods are useful on their own for contracts, but they become much more valuable when processed. Grain can become bread or whisky, and milk can become cheese. Fields and pastures cost more than workers but produce trade goods.

walkthrough.title: Unit Types: Processing Buildings
walkthrough.text: Processing buildings turn raw goods into refined products. Bakeries turn grain into bread. Dairies turn milk into cheese. Distilleries turn grain into whisky. During the Production phase, each processing building automatically converts one matching raw good. Refined goods are worth more on the market and are often required by export contracts. Building processors is essential for fulfilling high-value contracts.

walkthrough.title: Unit Types: Merchants
walkthrough.text: Merchants are special units that can be placed on any empty land hex. They do not produce goods, but they count for neighborhood bonuses and help you connect your network across the map. Merchants are useful for reaching distant hexes cheaply and filling gaps in your territory. They also count toward certain scoring tile conditions.

walkthrough.title: Action: Upgrade Shipping
walkthrough.text: The Upgrade Shipping action lets you increase your shipping level by one step. Pay the cost shown on your shipping track. Higher shipping levels let your units cross rivers and lochs when calculating placement distance. At shipping level zero, water blocks you entirely. Each level lets you cross one water boundary. This opens up the entire map and reduces distance costs dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Action: Fulfill an Export Contract
walkthrough.text: To fulfill an export contract, you must have the required goods shown on the contract tile. Pay the goods from your supply, then claim the contract. You receive the victory points printed on the tile, plus any bonus rewards like money, imported goods, or additional actions. Fulfilled contracts are placed near your player board and scored at game end. This is your primary source of victory points.

walkthrough.title: Action: Take an Export Contract
walkthrough.text: You can also spend an action just to take an export contract from the display and add it to your personal supply. You do not have to fulfill it immediately. This is useful for reserving a contract you are working toward, or for denying a contract to an opponent. However, unfulfilled contracts at game end are worth negative points, so do not hoard contracts you cannot complete.

walkthrough.title: Action: Pass
walkthrough.text: When you cannot or do not want to take any more actions, you pass. Once you pass, you are out for the rest of the Action phase. You cannot come back. The remaining players continue taking actions until everyone has passed. The last player to pass can take multiple actions in a row since nobody else is left. Passing early gives you a better position in turn order for the next round because you likely have less money.

walkthrough.title: Production Phase
walkthrough.text: After everyone passes, the Production phase begins. All units on the map produce simultaneously. Workers produce their resource. Fields produce grain. Pastures produce wool or milk. Processing buildings each convert one raw good into a refined product. If you have a bakery but no grain, it produces nothing. Make sure your production chain is complete before building processors.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Phase
walkthrough.text: After production, check the scoring tile for the current round. Everyone scores bonus victory points based on the condition shown. Scoring tiles reward things like having the most workers, the most processed goods, or the best neighborhood connections. Track your points on the scoring track. After scoring, the round ends and a new round begins with the Preparation phase.

walkthrough.title: Neighborhood Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Whenever you place a unit adjacent to another player's unit, both players receive a neighborhood bonus. You gain money based on the type of neighboring unit. These bonuses encourage building near opponents rather than in isolation. The bonuses are printed on the player board for reference. Neighborhood bonuses trigger immediately when the unit is placed.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the fifth round's Scoring phase. Final scoring includes: victory points from fulfilled export contracts, points from the five round scoring tiles already tracked, remaining money converted at five pounds per one victory point, and settlement bonuses for connected groups of units. Subtract points for any unfulfilled contracts you took. The player with the most victory points wins. Ties go to the player with the most money remaining.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Highlands
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Clans of Caledonia! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four players. Each player has chosen a different clan with unique powers. Follow along with the moves, even if you have fewer players at your table. Let's dive in.

walkthrough.title: Round One Setup Recap
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their clan, their player board full of units, starting money, and two units already on the map from the initial placement. The five scoring tiles are revealed, and the first round's tile is visible. The export contracts are laid out. Turn order is set with the poorest player going first. Let's begin the Action phase.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Place a Grain Field
walkthrough.text: Player one goes first because they have the least money. They look at the map and place a grain field on a grassland hex right next to one of their starting units. Adjacent placement means zero distance cost, so they only pay the base cost from their player board. Smart move. The grain field will produce grain during the Production phase.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Place a Sheep Pasture
walkthrough.text: Player two places a sheep pasture on a hex adjacent to their starting woodcutter. Again, the distance cost is zero because it is right next door. Sheep will produce wool during Production. Player two is already thinking about export contracts that need wool. Building next to your existing units keeps costs low and your network tight.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: The Overspending Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player three spots a perfect mountain hex across the map, far from their starting units. They place a miner there, paying the base cost plus a hefty distance fee of eight pounds. That is nearly half their remaining money gone in one placement. Meanwhile, there was an open mountain hex just two spaces from their starting position that would have cost a fraction of the price.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Expand Adjacently
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is clear. Always check for adjacent or near-adjacent hexes before reaching across the map. Distance costs add up fast and can cripple your economy. In the early rounds, build outward from your starting cluster one or two hexes at a time. Save the big expansions for later when you have upgraded shipping and more money to spare.

walkthrough.title: Player Four: Trade Action
walkthrough.text: Player four decides to trade on the market. They sell one wool at the current market price, collecting the money. The wool price drops by one step on the market track. Then they use that money to buy one grain, which raises the grain price by one step. In a single Trade action, player four converted wool into grain to prepare for a contract. Notice how both transactions happened in one action.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Market Timing
walkthrough.text: The market is one of the most important systems in the game. Selling when prices are high and buying when prices are low can save you a fortune. Pay attention to what other players are producing. If three players are all making grain, the price will drop when they sell. If nobody is making whisky, it stays expensive. Timing your trades around other players' production cycles is key.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Take an Export Contract
walkthrough.text: Player one uses their second action to take an export contract from the display. The contract requires two bread and one cheese, and it is worth a big chunk of victory points. Player one does not have these goods yet, but they are building toward them with their grain field. Taking a contract early reserves it before someone else grabs it.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Upgrade Shipping
walkthrough.text: Player two pays the cost to upgrade their shipping level from zero to one. Now water boundaries count as only one distance step instead of completely blocking them. This opens up several good hexes across a nearby river. Upgrading shipping early can save you more money in future placements than it costs right now.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: The Scoring Tile Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player three takes another placement action. They place a second worker on the map. But here is the problem: this round's scoring tile awards bonus points for the most processed goods. Player three has no processing buildings and no plan to build any this round. They are completely ignoring the scoring tile and will score zero bonus points while everyone else picks up easy victory points.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read the Scoring Tiles
walkthrough.text: Every round has a scoring tile, and ignoring it is like leaving free money on the table. Before you take your first action each round, read the scoring tile and ask yourself what you can do to score well. Even a few points per round add up to fifteen or twenty points over five rounds. Plan your actions around the current scoring condition, not just your long-term strategy.

walkthrough.title: Player Four: Place a Cattle Pasture
walkthrough.text: Player four places a cattle pasture adjacent to their existing units. Cattle will produce milk during Production. Player four is thinking ahead. They already have a contract that needs cheese, and cheese comes from processing milk. They are building toward their processing chain step by step.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Place a Bakery
walkthrough.text: Player one places a bakery on the map adjacent to their grain field. During Production, the bakery will convert one grain into bread. Player one is building their processing chain: grain field produces grain, bakery turns grain into bread. They need bread for their export contract. This is smart, sequential engine building.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: The Processing Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player three has two grain fields and a wool-producing sheep pasture, but no processing buildings at all. They have been focused on raw production and expansion. Now they look at the export contracts and realize the high-value ones all require processed goods like bread, whisky, or cheese. Without bakeries or distilleries, player three can only sell raw goods on the market for mediocre returns.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Processing Early
walkthrough.text: Raw goods are fine for market trading, but processed goods are where the real value is. Bread, cheese, and whisky sell for more on the market and are required by the highest-value export contracts. Try to have at least one processing building by the end of round one or early round two. Without processors, you are leaving the most lucrative contracts to your opponents.

walkthrough.title: More Actions and Passing
walkthrough.text: The round continues with players taking more actions. Player two places a dairy to process milk into cheese. Player four fulfills a small export contract, paying goods and collecting victory points plus a money bonus. Player three finally places a distillery, better late than never. As players run low on money, they begin to pass one by one. Remember, once you pass you are done for the round.

walkthrough.title: Production Phase
walkthrough.text: Now all players have passed, so we enter the Production phase. Every unit on the map produces simultaneously. Grain fields produce grain. Sheep produce wool. Cattle produce milk. Player one's bakery converts one grain into bread. Player two's dairy converts one milk into cheese. Player three's distillery converts one grain into whisky. Check your goods supply and make sure you collected everything.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Phase
walkthrough.text: After production, we score the round. Check the Round one scoring tile. Everyone scores bonus points based on the condition shown. Players who planned for the scoring tile earn extra victory points. Player three, who ignored the tile, scores zero bonus points this round. Update the scoring track for all players.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Recovery Plan
walkthrough.text: Player three made three mistakes this round: overspending on a distant placement, ignoring the scoring tile, and building no processing until too late. But the game has four more rounds. The recovery plan is simple. Next round, focus on adjacent expansion, read the scoring tile before acting, and make sure your processing chain is complete. One bad round does not end the game.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Round two begins with the Preparation phase. Turn order updates based on current money. New export contracts fill the display. The second scoring tile is now active. Your production chain is starting to generate goods, so this round you can focus on fulfilling contracts and expanding strategically. The engine you built in round one pays off now.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through your first round of Clans of Caledonia. You have seen all six action types, watched the market fluctuate, produced goods, processed them into refined products, and scored a round. You also saw Player three learn from three common mistakes: overspending on distance, ignoring scoring tiles, and neglecting processing buildings. You are ready to continue on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Go
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all unit types, market rules, and clan powers.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the most common mistakes new players make. First, overspending on distant placements when adjacent hexes are available. Second, ignoring the round scoring tiles and missing easy bonus points. Third, not building processing buildings and being stuck with raw goods that cannot fulfill high-value contracts. Fourth, hoarding export contracts you cannot complete, which costs you negative points. Fifth, selling goods on the market when prices are low instead of waiting for a better moment. Keep these in mind and you will be ahead of most first-time players.

walkthrough.title: Money Management
walkthrough.text: Money is the lifeblood of Clans of Caledonia. Every action costs money, but having less money means better turn order. This creates a constant tension. You want to spend efficiently, not hoard. The best players spend almost everything each round on productive investments, ending with just enough to go first or second in turn order. Avoid sitting on a pile of cash doing nothing. Remember that leftover money converts to points at only five pounds per victory point, which is a terrible rate.

walkthrough.title: Expand Smartly
walkthrough.text: Placement is the biggest money sink in the game. Always expand outward from your existing network to minimize distance costs. Upgrade shipping early if water is blocking your expansion path. Think two placements ahead: where do you want to be at the end of the round, and what is the cheapest path to get there? Port bonuses can also reward specific placements, so check which ports are near your territory and plan to trigger their bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Processing Chain
walkthrough.text: Raw goods are useful, but processed goods win games. A grain field paired with a bakery or distillery is far more valuable than a grain field alone. Bread, cheese, and whisky sell for more on the market and are required by the best export contracts. Aim to have at least one processing building by the end of round one. By mid-game, you want a complete chain: raw producers feeding into processors. Think of it as an assembly line. Every missing link in the chain is lost value.

walkthrough.title: Play to Your Clan
walkthrough.text: Each of the nine clans has a unique power that nudges you toward a specific strategy. Lean into your clan's strength rather than fighting against it. If your clan gives a bonus for trading, trade more aggressively. If your clan reduces placement costs, expand faster than other players. Read your clan power carefully at the start and build your entire game plan around it. The clans are well balanced, so the advantage comes from maximizing your specific power, not from the power itself.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Tile Awareness
walkthrough.text: The five scoring tiles are visible from the start of the game. Take time at the beginning to read all five and plan your overall arc. If round three rewards having the most workers on the map, make sure you have workers placed by then. If round five rewards processed goods, make sure your chain is complete. The best players incorporate scoring tile conditions into every decision. Even scoring two or three extra points per round adds up to ten or fifteen points over the game.

walkthrough.title: Contract Strategy
walkthrough.text: Export contracts are your primary source of victory points. Take contracts early that match the goods you are already producing or plan to produce. Do not grab contracts speculatively unless you are confident you can fulfill them. Unfulfilled contracts cost you negative points. Prioritize contracts that give bonus rewards like cotton, tobacco, or extra money, since these bonuses fuel further actions. If you see an opponent close to fulfilling a lucrative contract, consider taking it yourself to deny them the points.

walkthrough.title: Market Timing
walkthrough.text: The dynamic market rewards players who pay attention to supply and demand. Sell goods when their price is high, which usually means selling something nobody else is producing. Buy goods when their price is low, typically right after other players have been selling. Watch what your opponents produce and anticipate when they will sell. If three players all produce grain, the grain price will plummet when they sell. Position yourself to take advantage of these price swings rather than fighting against them.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't overspend on distant placements — expand adjacently
Don't ignore round scoring tiles — easy bonus VP
Build processing buildings — raw goods can't fulfill top contracts
Don't hoard contracts you can't complete (negative VP)
Don't sell goods when market prices are low

summary.title: Money Management
bullets: Spend efficiently — sitting on cash wastes tempo
Less money = better turn order (useful tension)
Leftover money converts at a bad rate (5£ = 1VP)
Invest in productive placements, not hoarding

summary.title: Expand Smartly
bullets: Expand outward from existing network to minimize distance costs
Upgrade shipping early if water blocks your path
Plan 2 placements ahead — cheapest path to your goal
Check nearby port bonuses and plan to trigger them

summary.title: Build Your Processing Chain
bullets: Processed goods (bread, cheese, whisky) > raw goods
Aim for 1 processor by end of round 1
Complete chain by mid-game: raw → processed
Every missing link = lost market value and contract opportunity

summary.title: Play to Your Clan
bullets: Lean into your clan's unique power — don't fight it
Trading clan → trade aggressively; expansion clan → spread fast
Build your entire game plan around your clan ability
Advantage comes from maximizing your power, not the power itself

summary.title: Scoring Tile Awareness
bullets: All 5 scoring tiles visible from game start — read them all
Plan your arc across all 5 rounds
2-3 extra points/round = 10-15 points over the game
Incorporate scoring conditions into every decision

summary.title: Contract Strategy
bullets: Take contracts matching your production plan early
Don't grab speculatively — unfulfilled = negative VP
Prioritize contracts with bonus rewards (cotton, tobacco, money)
Consider denying key contracts from opponents

summary.title: Market Timing
bullets: Sell high (scarce goods), buy low (after others sell)
Watch what opponents produce to anticipate price drops
3 players producing grain → grain price plummets when they sell
Position to exploit price swings, not fight them

walkthrough.title: Whisky Export Engine
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful strategies in Clans of Caledonia is the whisky export engine. Grain fields feed distilleries, and whisky is one of the highest-value goods on the market and in export contracts. The idea is to build two or three grain fields with one or two distilleries, creating a steady supply of whisky each round. Use the whisky to fulfill high-value contracts or sell when the market price is favorable. This strategy pairs especially well with Clan Buchanan, which gains bonuses from whisky production.

walkthrough.title: Dairy and Cheese Domination
walkthrough.text: An alternative to whisky is focusing on the dairy chain. Cattle pastures produce milk, and dairies convert milk into cheese. Cheese commands high market prices and is required by many export contracts. The advantage of this strategy is that cattle are easier to place than multiple grain fields, since you only need one cattle pasture to feed one dairy. Pair cattle with sheep for wool diversity, and you can fulfill a wide variety of contracts.

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Expansion
walkthrough.text: Some clans reward territorial expansion. The aggressive expansion strategy focuses on placing many units quickly, taking advantage of neighborhood bonuses and controlling key hexes before opponents. Upgrade shipping early to cross water boundaries cheaply. Prioritize workers and merchants for their low base costs. This approach sacrifices early production efficiency for map control and position. It pays off in later rounds when your large network gives you cheap access to the entire map.

walkthrough.title: Market Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use the market not just for conversion but for profit. The key insight is that you can buy low and sell high within the same game, or even the same round. If opponents flood the market with grain, the price drops. Buy it cheap, then sell it later when the price recovers. You can also sell goods right before opponents need to buy them, driving the price up. Watch the export contracts others are working toward and sell the goods they need right before they buy.

walkthrough.title: Contract Chaining
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players do not fulfill contracts one at a time in isolation. They chain contracts together, using the bonus rewards from one contract to fuel the next. Some contracts give cotton or tobacco as bonuses, and those imported goods are required by other contracts. Plan your contract sequence so that fulfilling contract A gives you the bonus needed for contract B. This creates a cascading effect where each contract accelerates the next.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Turn order in Clans of Caledonia goes to the player with the least money. Going first is extremely valuable because you get first pick of contracts, market prices, and hex placements. Advanced players manipulate their cash reserves to ensure they go first in critical rounds. Spend aggressively in the current round's Action phase so you enter the next round with less money. The player who goes first in round five often has a significant advantage in grabbing the last high-value contracts.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Tile Optimization
walkthrough.text: The five scoring tiles are visible from the start, so plan your entire game arc around them. Identify which two or three tiles you can realistically score well on, and focus your efforts there. Do not try to win every scoring tile. Instead, pick your battles and invest heavily in the rounds where the tile matches your strategy. If a tile rewards processed goods in round three, make sure your processing chain is complete by then. If round five rewards settlement size, save an expansion push for late game.

walkthrough.title: Clan-Specific Power Maximization
walkthrough.text: Every clan has a power that becomes devastating when fully leveraged. Clan MacDonald gets cheaper unit placement, making aggressive expansion extremely efficient. Clan Campbell earns more from selling goods, turning the market into a profit machine. Clan MacKenzie gains extra shipping for free, opening the entire map early. Clan Buchanan profits from whisky, supercharging the distillery chain. The key is to identify what your clan does best and build every decision around amplifying that advantage. A mediocre strategy that maximizes your clan power will beat a strong strategy that ignores it.

entries.definition: A worker unit placed on forest hexes. Produces wood during the Production phase. One of the cheapest units to deploy. Wood is not a trade good but provides resources for your clan.
A worker unit placed on mountain hexes. Produces money during the Production phase. Miners are a direct income source that helps fund future placements and market trades.
A worker unit placed on hexes adjacent to rivers or lochs. Produces food during the Production phase. Fishermen provide sustenance that supports your clan's growth.
Placed on grassland hexes. Produces one grain per Production phase. Grain is a raw good that can be sold on the market or processed into bread (via bakery) or whisky (via distillery).
Placed on grassland hexes. Produces one wool per Production phase. Wool can be sold on the market or used to fulfill export contracts requiring wool.
Placed on grassland hexes. Produces one milk per Production phase. Milk can be sold on the market or processed into cheese via a dairy.
A processing building that converts one grain into one bread during the Production phase. Bread is a refined good worth more on the market and required by many export contracts.
A processing building that converts one milk into one cheese during the Production phase. Cheese is a high-value refined good on the market and in export contracts.
A processing building that converts one grain into one whisky during the Production phase. Whisky is the most valuable refined good and commands premium market prices.
A special unit that can be placed on any empty land hex. Does not produce goods but counts for neighborhood bonuses and helps connect your network across the map. Useful for reducing distance costs.
A raw good produced by grain fields. Can be sold on the market, used for export contracts, or processed into bread (bakery) or whisky (distillery).
A raw good produced by cattle pastures. Can be sold on the market, used for export contracts, or processed into cheese (dairy).
A raw good produced by sheep pastures. Can be sold on the market or used for export contracts. Wool has no processing chain — it is used as-is.
A refined good produced by bakeries from grain. Worth more than grain on the market. Required by many high-value export contracts.
A refined good produced by dairies from milk. High market value. Required by several lucrative export contracts.
A refined good produced by distilleries from grain. The most valuable trade good on the market. Required by premium export contracts.
An imported good that cannot be produced by any clan. Obtained only through export contract bonuses or market purchase. Required by certain export contracts.
An imported good that cannot be produced by any clan. Obtained only through export contract bonuses or market purchase. Required by certain export contracts.

The central market board where goods are bought and sold. Each good has a price track. Buying a good raises its price by one step. Selling a good lowers its price by one step. Prices are shared across all players and fluctuate throughout the game.

Each trade good (wheat, milk, wool, cheese, bread, whisky) has its own price track on the market board. A marker moves up when that good is bought and down when sold. All players buy and sell at the current marker position.

A tile showing required goods and reward (VP plus bonuses). Take a contract with one action, fulfill it with another by paying the goods. Fulfilled contracts are your primary VP source. Unfulfilled contracts at game end cost negative VP.

Many export contracts provide bonus rewards beyond VP when fulfilled. Common bonuses include money, cotton, tobacco, free unit placements, or additional actions. These bonuses can chain into fulfilling subsequent contracts.
Any export contract you have taken but not fulfilled by game end costs you negative victory points. Only take contracts you are confident you can complete. The penalty varies by contract.

Reduces the base cost of placing units on the map. Favors aggressive expansion strategies with many units placed quickly. Their cost advantage compounds over the game as they place more units than other clans.

Earns a bonus when selling goods on the market. Favors market-heavy strategies where you produce and sell frequently. Campbell turns the market into a profit engine rather than just a conversion tool.

Starts with a higher shipping level or gains shipping upgrades for free. Opens the entire map early, reducing distance costs when expanding across water. Favors strategies that spread across the map quickly.
Gains bonuses related to whisky production. Pairs naturally with the distillery chain strategy. Buchanan players should prioritize grain fields and distilleries for maximum clan synergy.

Five tiles drawn randomly from a pool of nine, one per round. Each tile specifies a condition that awards bonus VP at the end of that round. Conditions include most workers, most processed goods, largest settlement, most fulfilled contracts, and others. All five are visible from game start.

At game end, connected groups of your units on the map score settlement bonuses. Larger connected groups score more VP. Rivers and lochs break connections unless your shipping level allows crossing them. Keep your network connected for maximum settlement points.

At game end, remaining money converts to VP at a rate of five pounds per one victory point. This is a poor conversion rate, so it is better to spend money on productive actions during the game rather than hoarding it for end-game conversion.

A track on your player board ranging from zero to the maximum. Each level lets you cross one water boundary (river or loch) when calculating distance for unit placement. At level zero, water completely blocks expansion. Upgrading costs money but saves on future distance fees.

When placing a unit, you pay a distance cost based on how far the target hex is from your nearest existing unit. Adjacent hexes have zero distance cost. Each additional hex of distance increases the cost. Water boundaries block distance paths unless shipping is upgraded.

When you place a unit adjacent to an opponent's unit, both players receive a money bonus. The bonus amount depends on the type of neighboring unit. Encourages building near opponents rather than in isolation.

Determined at the start of each round. The player with the least money goes first. Ties broken by previous turn order. Going first gives priority access to contracts, market prices, and hex placements. A strategic advantage worth managing.

Grain field produces grain during Production. If you have a bakery, it converts one grain into one bread during the same Production phase. Bread is worth significantly more than grain on the market and in contracts.

Grain field produces grain during Production. If you have a distillery, it converts one grain into one whisky during the same Production phase. Whisky is the most valuable processed good. Note: a single grain cannot become both bread and whisky.

Cattle pasture produces milk during Production. If you have a dairy, it converts one milk into one cheese during the same Production phase. Cheese is a high-value good required by many export contracts.

Small tiles placed on port spaces around the map edge during setup. When you place a unit adjacent to a port, you receive the bonus shown on the tile. Bonuses vary each game and can include money, goods, VP, or free upgrades.
